sections.heading: Pathway
sections.content: Management of Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia (PeIN) or Ta Disease (branching_basis: Clinical stage, Patient willingness for close follow-up, Lesion characteristics). Criteria: Clinical diagnosis of PeIN or Ta (noninvasive). Options: 1. Topical therapy: Imiquimod 5% or fluorouracil 5% - Recommended: For PeIN or Ta, topical imiquimod applied at night three times per week for 4–16 weeks, or fluorouracil cream applied twice daily for 2–6 weeks [PN-A1]. 2. Surgical: Wide local excision or circumcision - Recommended: Wide local excision with negative margins; circumcision alone may suffice for distal prepuce tumors [PN-A2]. 3. Laser therapy: CO2, Nd:YAG, or KTP laser - Category 2B: For selected PeIN, Ta, or pT1a lesions; perioperative 3%–5% acetic acid application to identify HPV-infected areas [PN-A1]. 4. Surgical: Complete glansectomy - Category 2B: For distal tumors on glans or prepuce; not recommended for pT1a unless needed for negative margins [PN-A2]. 5. Surgical: Mohs micrographic surgery - Category 2B: Alternative to wide local excision in select cases; thin layers excised microscopically until negative for tumor [PN-A2]. Adjuvant logic: Does not apply.

sections.heading: Pathway
sections.content: Management of pT1a Primary Penile Cancer (branching_basis: Tumor size and location, Patient preference for organ preservation, Surgical feasibility). Criteria: Pathologic stage pT1a (invasion into lamina propria without lymphovascular/perineural invasion, not high grade). Options: 1. Surgical: Wide local excision - Recommended: With skin grafting if needed; careful consideration for organ preservation if patient can adhere to close follow-up [PN-2]. 2. Surgical: Glansectomy - In select cases: Only if required to ensure complete tumor eradication with negative margins [PN-A2]. 3. Surgical: Partial penectomy - If organ-sparing not feasible: When tumor location or size precludes conservative surgery; intraoperative frozen sections for margins [PN-B]. 4. Radiation therapy: Brachytherapy or EBRT - Category 2B: For penile preservation; circumcision first to prevent radiation complications [PN-C]. 5. Laser therapy: CO2 or Nd:YAG laser - Category 2B: For selected cases with acceptable outcomes [PN-A1]. Adjuvant logic: Does not apply.

sections.heading: Pathway
sections.content: Management of pT1b or T2 or Greater Primary Penile Cancer (branching_basis: Tumor stage and grade, Lymphovascular invasion, Depth of invasion, Nodal status). Criteria: Pathologic stage pT1b (lymphovascular/perineural invasion or high grade) or ≥T2 (invasion into corpus spongiosum or cavernosum). Options: 1. Surgical: Partial penectomy - Standard for high-grade tumors: If functional stump can be preserved with negative margins; intraoperative frozen sections [PN-B]. 2. Surgical: Total penectomy - If partial not possible: For extensive invasion into corpora cavernosum or when negative margins cannot be achieved with partial penectomy [PN-B]. 3. Radiation therapy: EBRT with or without chemotherapy - Category 2B or 3: For tumors <4 cm: brachytherapy alone or EBRT; for tumors ≥4 cm or node-positive: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy (category 3) after circumcision [PN-C]. 4. Combined modality: Chemoradiation - Category 3: EBRT 65–70 Gy with concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy for select cases [PN-C]. Adjuvant logic: Applies. Summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiation may be considered for high-risk features such as positive margins or nodal involvement.

flowcharts.title: Management of Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia (PeIN) or Ta Disease

nodes.label: Clinical diagnosis of PeIN or Ta (noninvasive)
For PeIN or Ta, topical imiquimod applied at night three times per week for 4–16 weeks, or fluorouracil cream applied twice daily for 2–6 weeks [PN-A1].
Wide local excision with negative margins; circumcision alone may suffice for distal prepuce tumors [PN-A2].
For selected PeIN, Ta, or pT1a lesions; perioperative 3%–5% acetic acid application to identify HPV-infected areas [PN-A1].
For distal tumors on glans or prepuce; not recommended for pT1a unless needed for negative margins [PN-A2].
Alternative to wide local excision in select cases; thin layers excised microscopically until negative for tumor [PN-A2].

flowcharts.title: Management of pT1a Primary Penile Cancer

nodes.label: Pathologic stage pT1a (invasion into lamina propria without lymphovascular/perineural invasion, not high grade)
With skin grafting if needed; careful consideration for organ preservation if patient can adhere to close follow-up [PN-2].
Only if required to ensure complete tumor eradication with negative margins [PN-A2].
When tumor location or size precludes conservative surgery; intraoperative frozen sections for margins [PN-B].
For penile preservation; circumcision first to prevent radiation complications [PN-C].
For selected cases with acceptable outcomes [PN-A1].

flowcharts.title: Management of pT1b or T2 or Greater Primary Penile Cancer

nodes.label: Pathologic stage pT1b (lymphovascular/perineural invasion or high grade) or ≥T2 (invasion into corpus spongiosum or cavernosum)
If functional stump can be preserved with negative margins; intraoperative frozen sections [PN-B].
For extensive invasion into corpora cavernosum or when negative margins cannot be achieved with partial penectomy [PN-B].
For tumors <4 cm: brachytherapy alone or EBRT; for tumors ≥4 cm or node-positive: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy (category 3) after circumcision [PN-C].
EBRT 65–70 Gy with concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy for select cases [PN-C].
Adjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiation may be considered for high-risk features such as positive margins or nodal involvement.

edges.label: Radiation therapy (EBRT with or without chemo)
